title: Leaf Drying Rack
desc: The fallen leaves are ready for sorting, but the drying rack has become a purple argument with gravity.

steps.text:
Indigo Grove's leaves are collected only after they fall on their own, which the apprentices insist means the tree has decided the color can be spared. Once gathered, the leaves are sorted by shade and dried on netted racks before being ground for dye, binding mixes, or whatever else the Conjurors decline to describe. This morning the lower drying rack sags at one corner and two trays have begun mingling shades that were never meant to meet.

The rack keeper holds a basket of fresh leaves against one hip. "Retension the drying net if you want the frame," she says. "Or sort the fallen leaves back by shade if you prefer the trays. Either way, I'd like the grove's output to stop looking like a fight between dusk and poor storage."

options.label: Retension the sagging drying net on the frame
Sort the leaves back into their proper shade trays

steps.text:
The drying net is woven fine enough to hold the smallest leaf without trapping the air it needs. One corner tie has loosened, leaving the whole rack with a droop that encourages leaves to slide together and colors to blur. You retie the corners, pull the mesh back to even tension, and check the frame by hand until the surface sits flat and patient above its supports once more.

The keeper spreads a fresh handful of dark violet leaves across the restored net and watches them settle without drifting downhill into the neighboring tray. That seems to restore her faith in the morning. "Good. Drying is already a slow argument. The rack does not need to pick sides."

options.label: Set the tie cord away and leave the net level

steps.text:
The trays are sorted by tone: blue-violet, deep purple, near-black indigo, and the rare leaves that dry to a smoky silver edge the Warpainters prize for their own reasons. Wind and one clumsy hand have mixed the middle two piles into a muddled compromise. You separate them leaf by leaf, reading the color from vein to tip and returning each one to the tray where it belongs.

By the time you finish, the rack looks less like a spill and more like a study in controlled variations of the same strange hue. The keeper glances over the rows and relaxes slightly. "Better. The grove makes enough mysteries. Shade sorting should remain solvable."

options.label: Brush the last stray leaf into its tray and step back
